meta.title: Plan a 3-day Tokyo trip
meta.description: A research-heavy task too big for one window. The agent fills its context with travel research, notices it's running out of room, compacts the research into a smaller (and lossier) summary, writes durable notes to a file, and runs out of session. The next day, in a fresh empty window, it reads those notes back and finishes the plan — recovering the one detail the summary dropped. Token counts are hand-tuned estimates.

files.README.txt: This run plans a trip across two work sessions. The agent's durable notes appear here as files as it writes them — first the working notes, then the finished itinerary.

events.summary: You are a travel-planning assistant. Research thoroughly before you plan. When the context window gets full, compress what you've learned and save durable notes to a file so work can continue in a later session.

events.text: Help me plan a 3-day Tokyo trip in November. I like food markets and quiet temples, and I really don't want to cram — keep the days relaxed.

Three days, November, food markets and quiet temples, and an explicit ask to keep it unhurried. I'll research neighborhoods, a market, and some calm temples before I commit to a shape — better to read a few good pages than plan from memory.

events.text: The neighborhood guide, the market page, the quiet-temples list, and the November overview all look substantive and match what they asked for. The blitz blog is the opposite of relaxed — thirty stops in three days — so I'll skip it. I'll read the other four.

events.extracted: Yanaka is one of the few Tokyo neighborhoods that survived the 20th century largely intact — low wooden houses, narrow lanes, and dozens of small temples packed into a walkable grid. It sits beside Nezu and Sendagi (together called 'Yanesen'), an area locals treat as a quiet escape. Start at Yanaka Ginza, a modest shopping street, then wander the temple lanes toward Nezu Shrine. It rewards slow walking rather than a checklist. Mornings and late afternoons are calmest; midday tour groups pass through Yanaka Ginza but rarely the side streets.

events.text: Yanaka is exactly the register they want — calm, walkable, temple-dense, no rush. That's a strong anchor for one day. Now the temples themselves, for specifics.

events.extracted: Nezu Shrine is one of Tokyo's calmest, open 9:30 to 16:30 with the last entry at 16:00; it's busiest at weekends. Gotokuji, the temple famous for its rows of lucky-cat figures, is open 8:00 to 16:30 daily and sits a little west of the center. Several smaller temples in the area close their inner halls earlier midweek — often by mid-afternoon on Wednesdays — so it's worth checking a specific temple's hours before a long trip across town.

Tsukiji's inner wholesale market moved to Toyosu years ago, but the Outer Market — the streets of food stalls, knife shops, and small restaurants — stays where it always was and is very much alive. Come hungry and come early: most stalls open around 8:00 and the best ones wind down by early afternoon. Weekday mornings are the sweet spot; Sundays and public holidays are crowded, and a scattering of stalls close on Wednesdays. Look for grilled seafood skewers, tamagoyaki on a stick, and fresh sushi breakfasts.

events.extracted: November is one of the best months to visit Tokyo: cool, dry, and clear, with daytime highs around 15°C. It's the tail of autumn — the city's ginkgo avenues and temple gardens turn gold and red, usually peaking in the second half of the month. Crowds are lighter than in cherry-blossom season. Pack layers for cool evenings, and note that some gardens extend their hours for evening foliage light-ups late in the month.

events.text: I'm running low on room — the window is nearly full of raw research and I can feel the earliest of it about to get pushed out. Before I lose anything, let me compress what I've learned into a short digest and then save proper notes to a file, so a later session can pick this up cleanly.

events.summary: Research digest. Quiet temples: focus on Yanaka & Nezu — a walkable old-town area of small temples and narrow lanes, calmest morning and late afternoon. Gotokuji (the lucky-cat temple) is worth adding, a little west of center. Food: Tsukiji Outer Market, best in the morning before stalls wind down; some close Wednesdays. Season: November is mild with late-autumn foliage peaking mid-to-late month, crowds light. One caution carried over from the temple guide: some temples close early midweek, so exact opening hours need checking before building a day around them.

events.content:
# Tokyo trip — working notes

## Preferences
- 3 days, November. Likes food markets and quiet temples.
- Does NOT want to cram — keep every day light and unhurried.

## Chosen areas
- Tsukiji Outer Market — food; go early (before ~10am), some stalls closed midweek.
- Yanaka & Nezu — quiet old-town temples and shrines, walkable and calm.
- Gotokuji — the lucky-cat temple, a little west of center, for a calm second day.

## Day shape so far
- Day 1: Tsukiji market in the morning, Yanaka temples in the afternoon. (drafted)
- Days 2-3: still to plan — keep them slow.

## Open questions
- Exact opening hours for Nezu Shrine and Gotokuji. The research summary only kept "some temples close early midweek" and dropped the specific times — look them up before finalizing.

events.text: Good start — here's where day 1 landed. Morning at Tsukiji Outer Market for breakfast and stalls, then a slow afternoon wandering Yanaka's temple lanes toward Nezu Shrine. It fits your "don't cram" rule with room to spare. I've saved my research and this draft to a notes file, so we can pick it up anytime — I'll plan days 2 and 3 next.
This is a new session — my window is empty and I don't actually remember our earlier conversation. But I left myself notes last time. Let me read them before I do anything else.

events.text: That's enough to continue: day 1 is set, the preferences are clear, and I flagged one open question to myself — the exact temple hours, which my earlier summary dropped. I'll look those up now so I can place days 2 and 3 without anyone having to rush.

events.extracted: Official visiting hours. Nezu Shrine: 9:30-16:30 for the grounds, last entry 16:00. Gotokuji: 8:00-16:30, open daily. Note: on the third Wednesday of each month, Nezu's inner hall closes early at 14:00 for cleaning.

events.text: There they are — Nezu 9:30 to 16:30, Gotokuji 8:00 to 16:30. That's the detail the summary lost, recovered in one lookup. Now I can build the rest around real times instead of guessing.

Shape for the rest, keeping it slow: day 2 is an unhurried temple day out west — Gotokuji in the calm of the morning, then nothing fixed. Day 3 is Kagurazaka's quiet sloped streets with a late start and an open evening. Three gentle days, no cramming.

events.content:
# Tokyo - 3 days in November

## Day 1 - Tsukiji & Yanaka
- Morning: Tsukiji Outer Market, arrive by 9am while stalls are busy and fresh.
- Afternoon: walk Yanaka's old-town lanes to Nezu Shrine (9:30-16:30, last entry 16:00).
- Evening: an easy dinner near your hotel. Nothing scheduled.

## Day 2 - Temples, unhurried
- Late morning: Gotokuji (8:00-16:30), the calm lucky-cat temple out west.
- Afternoon: nothing fixed - tea, a bookshop, a slow wander.

## Day 3 - Kagurazaka
- Morning: sleep in.
- Midday: Kagurazaka's sloped backstreets and small shops.
- Keep the evening open for your flight or one last meal.

events.text: Here's the finished plan. Day 1 is Tsukiji Outer Market in the morning, then Yanaka's quiet temple lanes to Nezu Shrine in the afternoon. Day 2 is an unhurried temple morning at Gotokuji, the lucky-cat temple, with the rest of the day left open. Day 3 is a slow start and a wander through Kagurazaka's backstreets. I confirmed the temple hours before finalizing: Nezu is open 9:30-16:30 and Gotokuji 8:00-16:30. It's three gentle days with nothing crammed, and I've written the whole thing to itinerary.md.
